coredata.dc:title: Ultrathin layered MoS2/N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) heterostructures for highly sensitive room temperature NO2 gas sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Malathi, B.
Aysha Parveen, R.
Nakamura, A.
Archana, J.
Navaneethan, M.
Harish, S.
coredata.dc:description: Due to high surface-to-volume ratio, stability, and more active sites, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) has been attracted as a promising gas-sensing material. However, the room temperature (RT- 30 ℃) MoS2-based gas sensors still suffer slow response, recovery rates, and less sensitivity, notably for NO2. Herewith, we synthesized a novel molybdenum disulfide/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (MoS2/NGQD) heterostructure via hydrothermal method for NO2 detection. The MNG-3 heterostructure is endowed with enhanced response of 82.4% towards 50 ppm of NO2 at 30 ℃, which is 6.5 folds greater than that of pristine MoS2 nanosheets (NSs). MNG-3 samples outperform with a quick recovery (30 s), excellent repeatability (5 cycles), long-term stability (5 weeks), and good selectivity towards 30 ℃ NO2 detection concentration of 50 ppm. The unique 2D/0D heterostructures and synergistic effect of NGQD and MoS2 NSs are responsible for the improved sensing performance. It undoubtedly suggests the feasible nature of prepared MoS2/NGQD heterostructures for 30 ℃ NO2 detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heterostructure
Nanosheets
NO2 sensor
MoS2/NGQD
Room temperature (30 ℃)
Quick recovery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD)
Synthesis of pristine MoS2 and MoS2/NGQD heterostructure

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: SRM Institute of Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: SRM Institute of Science and Technology
Japan Society for the Promotion of Science

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: J. Archana is currently working as an Associate Professor (Research) in the Department of Physics and Nanotechnology, SRM IST, India. She has obtained her B.Sc., from Madurai Kamaraj University / Lady Doak College, Madurai, India and M.Sc., in Physics from Madurai Kamaraj University / NMSSVN College, Madurai, India and a Ph.D. in Physics from SRM University, India. She has published more than 120 research papers international journals and over 100 presentations in national / international conferences. She was awarded the prestigious JSPS invitational Award - Shizuoka University, Japan, August 2022, Mother Teresa Woman Scientist Award - mother Teresa university, Feb 2022, Visiting professor - Shizuoka University, Japan – 2019, Young Scientists Research Award - DAE-BRNS – 2017, JSPS Fellowship Award, JSPS Government of Japan. 2013 – 2015. Outstanding Researcher Award, Shizuoka University, Japan 2013, Horri Award for Excellence in Research, Shizuoka University, Japan 2013, Monbukagusho Japanese government fellowship, 2011 – 2013, Young Researcher Award, ASIA NANO 2010, Tokyo, Japan 2010. Her research interests are nanomaterials-based devices such as Dye-sensitized solar cells, Photovoltaics, Photocatalysis, Gas sensors and Thermoelectric materials.
The authors thank the management of SRM Institute of Science and Technology for the support through SEED, STARTUP grant, and Nanotechnology Research Center (NRC) for the research facilities.

S. Harish is currently working as an Assistant Professor in the Department of Physics and Nanotechnology, SRM IST, India. He has obtained his B.Sc., and M.Sc., in Physics from Madras University, India and a Ph.D. in Physics from SRM University, India. He has published more than 170 research papers international journals and over 100 presentations in national / international conferences. He was awarded the prestigious JASSO Research Fellowship of the Japanese government (2015–2016) & (2017–2018). He was received several awards including the Dean Award for outstanding contribution to Academic research, Shizuoka University, Japan, 2018 and Research grant for overseas travel award, Awarded Murata Science Foundation, Japan GEET 2019, Paris, France. His research interests are nanomaterials-based devices such as Dye-sensitized solar cells, Photovoltaics, Photocatalysis, Gas sensors and Thermoelectric materials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern of pristine MoS2, and MoS2/NGQD heterostructures, (b) Enlarged image of (002) peak.
FESEM image of (a) Pristine MoS2, (b-e) MoS2/NGQD heterostructures, (f-j) Elemental distribution of MNG-3 sample.
TEM and HRTEM image of (a-c) Pristine MoS2 and (d-f) MNG-3 heterostructures.
(a&c) Molybdenum 3d, (b&d)) Sulfur 2p core level spectra of MNG-0 & MNG-3, e) Carbon 1 s and f) Nitrogen 1 s core level spectra of MNG-3.
(a-e) Transient resistance curves of MoS2/NGQD towards (10–50 ppm) NO2 at 30 ℃ (f) Response of the sensors towards 50 ppm NO2 at varying operating temperature.
(a) Response of MoS2/NGQD sensor towards (10–50 ppm NO2) at 30 ℃, (b) Response and resistance of MNG-3 towards varying operating temperature, (c & d) Response and recovery rates of MNG-0 and MNG-3.
(a) Repeatability, (b) stability of MNG-3, (c) Anti-humidity effect of MoS2/NGQD heterostructures at 30 ℃.
(a) Limit of detection (LOD) of MNG-3, (b) Selectivity of fabricated sensors towards different gases.
Comparison of previously reported MoS2-based sensors and our fabricated sensor.
Schematic representation of gas sensing mechanism of MoS2/NGQD.

head.ce:title: Ultrathin layered MoS2/N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) heterostructures for highly sensitive room temperature NO2 gas sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Functional Materials and Energy Devices Laboratory, Department of Physics and Nanotechnology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Chennai 603203, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School of Science and Technology, Shizuoka University, 3-5-1 Johoku, Naka-Ku, Hamamatsu, Shizuoka 432-8011, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate School of Science and Technology, Shizuoka University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Graduate School of Science and Technology, Shizuoka University, 3–5-1 Johoku, Naka-Ku, Hamamatsu, Shizuoka 432–8011, Japan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Center (NRC), Faculty of Engineering and Technology, SRM institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Chennai 603203, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanotechnology Research Center (NRC), Faculty of Engineering and Technology, SRM institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology Research Center (NRC), Faculty of Engineering and Technology, SRM institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, India, 603203

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to high surface-to-volume ratio, stability, and more active sites, molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) has been attracted as a promising gas-sensing material. However, the room temperature (RT- 30 ℃) MoS2-based gas sensors still suffer slow response, recovery rates, and less sensitivity, notably for NO2. Herewith, we synthesized a novel molybdenum disulfide/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (MoS2/NGQD) heterostructure via hydrothermal method for NO2 detection. The MNG-3 heterostructure is endowed with enhanced response of 82.4% towards 50 ppm of NO2 at 30 ℃, which is 6.5 folds greater than that of pristine MoS2 nanosheets (NSs). MNG-3 samples outperform with a quick recovery (30 s), excellent repeatability (5 cycles), long-term stability (5 weeks), and good selectivity towards 30 ℃ NO2 detection concentration of 50 ppm. The unique 2D/0D heterostructures and synergistic effect of NGQD and MoS2 NSs are responsible for the improved sensing performance. It undoubtedly suggests the feasible nature of prepared MoS2/NGQD heterostructures for 30 ℃ NO2 detection.

ce:section.ce:para: The growing industrial trends and urbanization have comforted human lives however, it leads to the emission of several toxic gases into the atmosphere. Among them, NO2 demands immense attention in detection because of its abundance and highly harmful impact on human lives even in its trace quantities [1]. Further, NO2 exposure leads to several health effects including headache, eye irritation, cardiac arrest, asthma, and respiratory disorders [2–9]. Therefore, it is crucial for humans to have early and sensitive NO2 detection even at its low concentrations [10].

Many researchers have recently reported on the gas-sensing capabilities of MoS2 with various operating conditions. For example, Li et al. reported a sensitivity of ∼21.5% for 100 ppm of NO2 at 100 ℃ with MoS2 microspheres having response and recovery time of 79 and 225 s, respectively [11]. Kumar et al. studied MoS2-based reversible NO2 sensor at 30 ℃ achieved ∼21.5% response towards 100 ppm with a recovery rate of 249 s [12]. Shao et al. achieved ∼ 2.2% of response with response and recovery rate of 24 and 34 s towards 10 ppm of NO2 via rGO-MoS2-CdS [13]. Cui et al. reported 30 ℃ NO2 detection with 28% response for 100 ppm of NO2 via SnO2 stabilized MoS2 nanosheets (NSs) [14]. Keerthi et al. reported La-MoS2 hybrid heterostructure with 98.07% sensitivity towards NO2 at 25 ppm with 89 and 95 s response and recovery rates [23]. Bai et al. studied CuO nanosphere with MoS2 nanosheet showing response of 8.9 for 10 ppm at 30 ℃ with response and recovery time of 18.5 and 53.5 s, respectively [24]. Zhou et al. reported MoS2/ZnO nanowires showing a sensitivity of 31.2% to 50 ppm of NO2 at 200 ℃ with slow response and recovery rates of about 1 h [25]. Although there have been several reports on MoS2-based NO2 detection with a higher response, the cumulative enhancement on sensitivity along with fast response recovery rates at low operating temperatures is still unattained [15]. Therefore, the nanocomposites of MoS2 with low dimensional carbon materials like graphene, reduced graphene oxide (rGO), and carbon nanotubes (CNT) have been explored to attain high sensitivity at low operating temperatures (OT) with rapid response and recovery rates. Zhou et al. reported rGO/MoS2 nanocomposite with ∼59.8% response towards 2 ppm of NO2 at 60 ℃ [16]. Hong et al. have studied MoS2/graphene two-dimensional heterostructure to detect NO2 (0.2–100 ppm) at 200 ℃ with rapid response and recovery of < 1 s [17]. Sangeetha et al. reported ∼61% response for 30 ℃ NO2 detection via MoS2/graphene-based hybrid structure with response and recovery time of 22 and 35 s respectively [18]. Ding et al. reported a 30 ℃ NO2 sensor of 14.8% response towards 500 ppb via CuO2 loaded with MoS2/rGO [26]. Wan et al. prepared In(OH) modified reduced graphene oxide for 30 ℃ NO2 detection and achieved a sensitivity of 19.79% for 1 ppm [27]. From the literature, it is evident that MoS2/low dimensional graphene heterostructures are suitable for attaining highly sensitive NO2 detection with rapid response and recovery at low operating temperatures, which is attributed by excellent charge transfer paths between MoS2 and graphene [19,20]. Especially, doped-low dimensional graphene is highly noted for attaining higher adsorbate binding energy and charge transfer at the reactive sites. Lv et al. synthesized the N-doped graphene quantum dots/SnO2 hierarchical hollow cube and reported a response of 417 towards 1 ppm of NO2 at 130 ℃ [21]. Lv et al. attained a response of 81.7 towards 1 ppm of NO2 at 100 ℃ with fast response (27 s) and recovery rates (10 s) with In2O3 decorated N-doped graphene quantum dots [22]. However, up to our knowledge, there are no reports on MoS2/N-doped graphene QDs for NO2 detection. Therefore, in this study, we developed MoS2/NGQD heterostructure via a facile hydrothermal method to explore the synergistic effect of 2D MoS2 and NGQD for 30 ℃ NO2 detection. The crystallinity, morphology, and chemical composition of the prepared MoS2/NGQD heterostructures were examined using XRD, FESEM, and XPS analysis respectively and the 30 ℃ NO2 sensing performance is evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD)
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid (0.2 g) and urea (1.8 g) were dissolved in 30 mL of distilled water with a continuous stir for 1 h. The resultant solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 160 °C for 4 h and then allowed to cool down naturally. The obtained liquid solution is nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pristine MoS2 and MoS2/NGQD heterostructure
ce:section.ce:para: In the synthesis of pristine MoS2, 2.5 g of hexaammonium heptamolybdate tetrahydrate was dissolved in 72 mL of DI water and stirred for 45 min, 4.5 g of thiourea was added to the above-dissolved solution and stirred for 45 min. The final solution was transferred into an autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 24 h. The obtained product was washed with DI water and ethanol several times and dried at 80 ℃ for 24 h. Further annealed at 300 °C for 2 h in nitrogen environment to obtain MoS2 nanosheets (NSs) and labeled as MNG-0. To synthesize heterostructures of MoS2 with N-doped graphene QDs (MoS2/NGQD), the same procedure of pristine MoS2 NSs synthesis was used with the addition of different volume of NGQD solution (10, 20, 30, 40 mL). The obtained powder was termed as MNG-1, MNG-2, MNG-3, and MNG-4.

The sensing measurements were carried out in a homemade gas sensing setup comprising a sensing chamber of volume 2500 cc integrated with a 34465 A Fluke multimeter paired with a data gathering system. The sensing chamber holds an inbuilt heater on the sensor stage and a pair of tungsten electrodes. The target gases were purchased in the dry form in gas canisters of 1.6 L holding 10,000 ppm balanced with N2. The desired concentration of target gas was injected using an airtight syringe into the sensing chamber. The sensor analysis was carried at room temperature (RT = 30 ℃) to 150 ℃. Also, the relative humidity in the measurement chamber was varied from 30% to 60% and the effect of relative humidity on the sensor performance at room temperature was evaluated. The humidity inside the sensing chamber was monitored using an HTC-2 humidity sensor.

The sensing response (%) towards oxidizing gas was calculated using Eq. 1, and for the reducing gas the corresponding formula is provided in Eq. S1.
(1)
S
%
=
Ra − Rg
Rg
× 100
Where S (%) is the response % of the sensor, R
a is the resistance of the sensor in the ambient atmosphere, and R
g is the resistance of the sensor upon interaction with target gas.

Fig. 1(a) shows the XRD pattern of pristine MoS2 and MoS2/NGQD heterostructure. The XRD patterns of pristine MoS2 confirm the formation of hexagonal (2 H-phase) with high crystallinity. The well-defined peaks at 14.4°, 33.5°, 34.5°, 39.5°, and 60.2° matched with JCPDS card no. #37-1492, space group P63/mmc and indexed to lattice planes (002), (100), (101), (104), and (110), respectively. Upon the addition of NGQD, there are no additional diffraction peaks. However, there is a notable decrease in the intensity and broadening of diffraction peaks (Fig. 1.(b)) due to the presence of NGQD [23]. Also, the predominant peak (002) shows a shift towards a lower angle upon an increase in NGQD concentration attributed to structural disorder and dilution effect [24,25]. The dilution effect in MoS2/NGQD is attributed to the reduction in MoS2 concentration and simultaneous increase in the NGQD, thereby leading to a decrease in the intensity of MoS2 diffraction peaks [26]. Crystalline parameters of the prepared MoS2/NGQD heterostructure are calculated and shown in Table S1.

FESEM image of pristine MoS2 and MoS2/NGQD heterostructure ( Fig. 2(a-e)) shows uniform nanosheets (NSs) like morphology. The NSs-like morphology persisted with the addition of NGQD until MNG-3 however for MNG-4, nanoflower comprising an array of NSs was observed. Additionally, the edges of the NSs in MNG-0 are indistinct, but they become evident after the addition of NGQD. Compared to other samples, the MNG-2 and MNG-3 exhibit mostly unique and distinct edge sites. In the MoS2/NGQD heterostructure, the NSs with large contact surfaces and exposed edge sites can result in a high surface-to-volume ratio, boosting the adsorption and desorption process upon gas interaction. Additionally, the elemental mapping carried out for MNG-3 (Fig. 2(g-j)) confirms the uniform distribution of Mo, S, C, and N.

The TEM images of MNG-0 ( Fig. 3.(a & b)) and MNG-3 (Fig. 3.(d & e)) confirm the ultrathin nanosheets (NSs) like morphology in accordance with FESEM. The HRTEM images of MNG-0 and MNG-3 (Fig. 3.(c & f)) show the well-defined lattice fringes for MNG-0 and MNG-3. The interplanar spacing of d= 0.63 nm observed for MNG-0 decreased to d= 0.62 nm for MNG-3 representing a reduction in the interlayer spacing along the (002) plane of MoS2. The decrease in interlayer gap observed for MNG-3 is in accordance with the c value observed in XRD. The interlayer gaps in MoS2 NSs facilitate the diffusion of gas molecules thereby enhancing the sensor response however, it might limit the stability and recovery of the sensor in the long term due to strong gas-sensor interaction. Therefore, the smaller d
(002) and prominent exposed edges for MNG-3 can provide for enhanced sensor performance. Also, the NGQD (N-doped graphene quantum dots) appear as dark spots on the surface of MNG-3 NSs confirming the decoration of NGQD on the surface of MoS2 NSs (Fig. 3.(f)).

The elemental analysis and chemical states of the MoS2 and MoS2/NGQD samples were investigated using XPS. The survey spectra (Fig. S4.) of MNG-0 and MNG-3 confirm the presence of Mo, S, C, and N, and absence of any additional elements represents the high purity of prepared samples. The Mo 3d core level spectra of MNG-0 (Fig. 5.(a)) and MNG-3 (Fig. 5.(c)) show a pair of peaks representing Mo 3d
3/2 and Mo 3d
5/2 of predominant 4 + states with spin-orbit splitting of 3.6 eV [27]. In MNG-0, the Mo 3d
3/2 and Mo 3d
5/2 peaks lying at 232.75 and 229.15 eV represent the Mo4+ states and the peak at 236.65 eV represents the Mo6+ state arising because of partial surface oxidation [28]. In MNG-3, the Mo 3d
3/2 and Mo 3d
5/2 peaks appear at 231.9 and 228.8 eV denoting the Mo4+ state. The slight shift of 0.3 eV in peak towards lower binding energy is attributed to the changes in the electronic structure caused by charge transfer between NGQD and MoS2 [29]. The less intense peak at 226.25 eV and 225.8 eV in Mo 3d spectra of MNG-0 and MNG-3 denotes the S 2 s of Sulphur. However, the S 2p core level spectra were displayed in Fig. 4.(b&d). Therefore, the observed shifts in Mo 3d and S 2p spectra of MNG-3 are attributed to the influence of NGQD and the corresponding electron transports at the interface. Such interfacial effect and the reduction in surface oxidation observed for MNG-3 will facilitate enhanced charge transport mechanisms upon gas-sensor interaction and thereby for highly responsive, long-term stable detection.

Initially, all the sensors were tested to detect NO2 concentrations (10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 ppm) with varying operating temperatures (RT – 30 ℃) upto 150 ℃ with an interval of 50 ℃. The transient resistance curve for pristine and other heterostructures at various temperatures (30–150 ℃) towards 50 ppm NO2 is shown in Fig. S8(a-o). The transient resistance curves of all the fabricated sensors towards (10–50 ppm) NO2 at 30 ℃ and the corresponding response towards 50 ppm are provided in Fig. 5(a-e). All the sensors show relatively higher response at 30 ℃ compared to other temperatures.
Therefore, the 30 ℃ detection is optimum for the fabricated MoS2 and MoS2/NGQD sensors and further measurements were performed at 30 ℃.

The response of MoS2/NGQD sensors towards 10–50 ppm NO2 at 30 ℃ is shown in Fig. 6.(a) The MNG-3 provided the highest response of 82.4% for 50 ppm NO2 at 30 ℃ among all other sensors. The higher response obtained for the MNG-3 sensor is attributed to the increase in surface area as observed in BET and well-exposed edge sites as seen in FESEM. The response and the base resistance of the MNG-3 sensor towards 50 ppm NO2 at varying temperatures are provided in Fig. 6.(b). The resistance of the sensor decreases as the operating temperature increases from 30 ℃ to 150 °C, indicating the semiconductor behavior of the sensor. Fig. 6.(c & d) and Fig. S9(f) represents the response and recovery time of fabricated pristine MoS2 and MoS2/NGQD sensors at 30 ℃. The response time and recovery time are defined as the time it takes for a sensor to reach 90% of response upon exposure to target gas and time it takes to reach 10% of its base resistance upon removal of the target gas, respectively [30]. Pure MoS2 and MoS2/NGQD (MNG-1, MNG-2, MNG-3 and MNG-4) showed the response and recovery time of t90∼37.5 s, ∼75.5 s, ∼83.5 s, ∼30 s, ∼63.5 s and t10∼56.5 s, ∼174.5 s, ∼159.5 s, ∼82.5 s, ∼236 s respectively. Due to the interaction kinetics of gas molecules with different sensor surfaces, response-recovery times show different values [31]. It is observed among all the sensors, the MNG-3 sensor provided rapid response which is due to the predominant exposure of highly active edge sites and higher surface area. The MNG-3 sensor provided quick recovery compared to all other MoS2/NGQD sensors, however, its slightly sluggish compared to pristine MoS2. In MNG-3 the enormous adsorption of NO2 molecules on the highly active sensor surface increases the number of electrons exchanged between the sensor-gas interface leading to a quick and higher response. However, large numbers of molecules need to be removed from the surface during recovery, thereby prolonging the time for the sensor to regain its initial resistance. Therefore, MNG-3 is identified as best among other sensors in terms of both quick response and recovery rates. Further evaluation on repeatability, stability, anti-humidity, and selectivity was evaluated for the MNG-3 sensor at 30 ℃.

The repeatability of the pristine (MNG-0) and other heterostructure sensors (MNG-1 to MNG-4) was evaluated by exposing the sensor towards five consecutive cycles of 50 ppm NO2 as shown in Figs. 7.(a) & S10.(a-d). The sensor's base resistance (65 MΩ), as well as the response (82.4%), remained almost the same for repeated cycles ensuring high repeatability of the sensor. Due to continuous exposure to NO2, there is a slight variation in its response (%) attributed to variations in the reaction when applying the same concentration of highly oxidizing NO2 repeatedly. However, the MNG-3 sensor was confirmed to have high repeatability towards NO2 with a very low standard deviation of 1%. To examine long-term stability, MoS2/NGQD sensors were tested periodically towards 50 ppm of NO2 at 30 ℃ for five weeks (Fig. 7.(b)). Initially, the MNG-3 samples showed ∼82.35% response to the target gas, after the second exposure it increased its response to ∼83.11% due to the influence of external pollution, oxygen, and humidity which can create new electrostatic centers for gas adsorption [16]. However, the response decreased from week 3 and provided a response of ∼56.34% at week 5. Such reduction in response at prolonged usage is due to saturated surface-active sites attributed to higher NO2 adsorption energy which leads to hindrance in the sensor-gas interface. Even though the sensor response decreased upon long-term usage, the sensor can notably detect NO2 with a high response of 56.34% thereby ensuring the feasible and stable operation of the MNG-3 sensor. The anti-humidity effect of the fabricated sensors at RT (30 ℃) is evaluated by measuring the response towards 50 ppm NO2 at various humidity conditions (30–60%). All the sensors showed stable detection and ensured there is no influence of humidity on the observed sensor performance Fig. 7.(c).

Further, it is crucial for the sensor to detect even trace concentrations of the target gas. Therefore, the Limit of detection (LOD) of MNG-3 sensor is theoretically estimated using Eq. 2. The response of the MNG-3 sensor towards varying NO2 concentration (10, 20, 30, 40, 50 ppm) was fitted using a linear regression equation as shown in Fig. 8.(a). The slope obtained from the linear fitted equation and the corresponding standard deviation were used in Eq. 2 to estimate its LOD [32]. The MNG-3 sensor ensured a very low LOD of 6 ppm as shown in Eq. S3. for NO2 at 30 ℃.
(2)
LOD =
3 σ
s
where,
σ - standard deviation of the intercept.
s – slope of linear fitting.

The selectivity of all the sensors was tested upon exposure to various interfering gases NH3, SO2, and NO2. The response of MNG-0, MNG-1, MNG-2, MNG-3 and MNG-4 sensors towards NO2 (50 ppm), NH3 (100 ppm), and SO2 (100 ppm), at 30 ℃ is shown in Fig. 8.(b). Both the sensors provided highly selective detection towards NO2 compared to all other interfering gases which is due to the large adsorption energy, small kinetic diameter, and lesser ionization energy of NO2 [33]. The MNG-0 sensor provided selectivity factor of KNH3/ NO2 = 0.68, KNO2/ NO2 = 1, and KSO2/NO2 = 0.42, whereas the MNG-3 sensor ensured higher selectivity with KNH3/ NO2 = 0.11, KNO2/ NO2 = 1, and KSO2/NO2 = 0.08. The observed high selectivity for the MNG-3 sensor is attributed to the activation energy of the sensor, the diameter of gas molecules, surface reaction dynamics, and bond energy [34].
The MNG-0 possesses an activation energy of 41.23 kJm−1 and MNG-3 showed 40.26 kJm−1 (Fig. S11(a & b). The lower activation energy for MNG-3 further supports the higher response obtained at 30 ℃.

The observed results suggest MNG-3 sensor as a potential candidate for 30 ℃ NO2 detection. Table S2. and Fig. 9. show the comparative report on the present study with the previously reported NO2 gas sensors.

The NO2 detection of the fabricated pristine MoS2 and MoS2/NGQD sensors involves adsorption, redox reactions, and desorption processes. Both the pristine MoS2 as well as MoS2/NGQD heterostructures are hole-dominant semiconductors and their resistance decreased upon increasing the sensor operating temperature. The MoS2 and MoS2/NGQD sensors provided enhanced sensing performance at RT (30 ℃) compared to higher operating temperatures. At low operating temperatures, the adsorption of gas molecules on the sensor surface dominates the desorption process but, at higher temperatures the desorption rate dominates adsorption. This leads to a reduction in the sensing performance at higher temperatures. Therefore, here the detection mechanism of the sensors at RT (30 ℃), towards NO2 was systematically proposed. Upon exposing the sensor to the ambient atmosphere, the ionosorption of oxygen molecules takes place on the surface of the sensor. The ionosorption process leads to temperature-dependent oxygen ion formation. At temperatures below 100 ℃, the adsorbed oxygen molecules trap surface-free electrons from the sensor and form oxygen molecular ions as follows.
(3)
e
−
+
O
2 ( gas )
↔
< 100 ℃
O
2 ( ads )
−

This leads to an increase in the hole concentration thereby increasing p-type conductivity, shifting the Fermi level towards the conduction band, certainly reaching surface saturation and decreasing the sensor’s resistance to attain stable base resistance (Ra) [11].
In the presence of strongly oxidizing gas NO2, the adsorption of NO2 on the sensor surface is followed by electron trapping as follows.

In the exposure to NO2,
(4) NO2 (gas) + e- → NO2 - (ads)
(5) 2NO2 (gas) + O2 - (ads) → 2NO3 - (ads)

Such formation of NO3 - ions leads to further enhancement in the hole concentration and decrease of sensor resistance [30]. In the case of MoS2/NGQD heterostructures, the gas-to-sensor interaction involves the same process as mentioned for MoS2 sensor. However, the 2D/0D junction, morphology, and surface properties lead to enhancement in the sensor performance. The pristine MoS2 possesses 2D layered flakes which are widely reported for a higher surface-to-volume ratio however, the random alignment of flakes leads to less exposed edge sites [35]. Upon incorporation of NGQD, the MoS2 flakes gather to form nanoflowers with highly exposed edge sites thereby increasing the surface area of MNG-3 sensor as identified through BET analysis. The higher surface area will lead to an increase in the interaction sites between the sensor and the target gas molecules thereby providing enhancement in the sensor response. Further, the incorporation of 0D NGQDs on the surface of 2D MoS2 flakes as identified through TEM leads to the formation of interfacial 0D/2D junctions. Additionally, the bandgap of MoS2 and NGQD is 1.3 eV and 3.0 eV respectively. In a hetero nanostructure generated by p-type MoS2 and p-type NGQD, free electrons from MoS2 are transferred to NGQDs, and holes are transferred in the opposite direction until the fermi level attains equilibrium. As a result, an internal electric field developed at the junction between MoS2 and NGQDs, which led to band bending at depletion layers [36]. When MoS2/NGQDs were exposed to NO2 gas, electrons would be captured by NO2 gas molecules from MoS2 and lead to an increase in the hole carrier concentration in MoS2. Therefore, the Fermi level difference between NGQDs and MoS2 eventually causes to change and increase conductivity via NGQD’s electrons flowing through the interface being transferred to the MoS2 as shown in Fig. 10.

These 0D/2D junctions will have free-charge carriers on either side of it. These free charge carriers are easily available for the target gas adsorption process thereby leading to further enhancement in the sensor performance. Also, the NGQD material favors sensitivity with an excellent path for charge transport during the adsorption and redox process [32]. This synergistic effect of morphology, 0D/2D junctions, and excellent charge transfer paths influence the enhancement of overall sensor performance.

In summary, we investigated the synergistic effect of hydrothermally prepared 2D/0D MoS2/NGQD heterostructures for 30 ℃ NO2 detection. Excellent gas sensitivity of 82.4% is observed for MNG-3 sensor towards 50 ppm NO2 at 30 ℃. Further, the sensor ensured repeatable response, long term stability and high selectivity for NO2 detection with rapid response and recovery rates. The enhanced sensor performance observed for the MNG-3 sensor is attributed to the cumulative effect of higher surface-to-volume ratio, predominantly exposed edge sites, and 2D/0D interface of MoS2 NSs and NGQDs. Therefore, the prepared MoS2/NGQD heterostructure sensor is identified as an excellent probe for real-time NO2 detection at 30 ℃.

Archana J.: Formal analysis, Investigation. Navaneethan M.: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Santhanakrishnan Harish: Investigation, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Malathi B.: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Parveen R. Aysha: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Software. Nakamura A.: Formal analysis, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the management of SRM Institute of Science and Technology for the support through SEED, STARTUP grant, and Nanotechnology Research Center (NRC) for the research facilities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.135083.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: B. Malathi is currently pursuing a Double Doctoral Program under the guidance of Dr. S. Harish, Assistant Professor, SRM IST, India and Dr. Atsushi Nakamura, Associate Professor, Shizuoka University, Japan. She obtained a B.Sc., degree from Bharathidasan Government college for women (Affiliated to Pondicherry University), India and an M.Sc., degree from Kanchi Mamunivar Centre for Post graduate studies and research (Affiliated to Pondicherry University), India. She was awarded Monbukagusho Japanese government fellowship. Her research interest includes Gas sensors, Photocatalysis, Nanomaterial synthesis and characterization.

R. Aysha Parveen is currently pursuing a Double Doctoral Program under the guidance of Dr. S. Ponnusamy, Professor, SRM IST, India and Dr. Kazuhiko Hara, Professor, Shizuoka University, Japan. She obtained a B.Sc., degree from Gurunanak College (Affiliated to University of Madras), India and an M.Sc., degree from Madras Christian College (Affiliated to University of Madras), India. She was awarded Monbukagusho Japanese government fellowship. Her research interest includes Gas sensors, Photodetectors, Photocatalysis, Nanomaterial’s synthesis and characterization.

Atsushi Nakamura is currently working as Associate Professor, Graduate school of science and technology, Shizuoka University, Shizuoka. He has published more than 101 research papers international journals and presentations in national/international conferences. His research interests are nanomaterials-based devices such as ZnO-based EL devices, Oxide semiconductors for energy devices, and nanocarbon materials.

J. Archana is currently working as an Associate Professor (Research) in the Department of Physics and Nanotechnology, SRM IST, India. She has obtained her B.Sc., from Madurai Kamaraj University / Lady Doak College, Madurai, India and M.Sc., in Physics from Madurai Kamaraj University / NMSSVN College, Madurai, India and a Ph.D. in Physics from SRM University, India. She has published more than 120 research papers international journals and over 100 presentations in national / international conferences. She was awarded the prestigious JSPS invitational Award - Shizuoka University, Japan, August 2022, Mother Teresa Woman Scientist Award - mother Teresa university, Feb 2022, Visiting professor - Shizuoka University, Japan – 2019, Young Scientists Research Award - DAE-BRNS – 2017, JSPS Fellowship Award, JSPS Government of Japan. 2013 – 2015. Outstanding Researcher Award, Shizuoka University, Japan 2013, Horri Award for Excellence in Research, Shizuoka University, Japan 2013, Monbukagusho Japanese government fellowship, 2011 – 2013, Young Researcher Award, ASIA NANO 2010, Tokyo, Japan 2010. Her research interests are nanomaterials-based devices such as Dye-sensitized solar cells, Photovoltaics, Photocatalysis, Gas sensors and Thermoelectric materials.

M. Navaneethan is currently working as an Associate Professor (Research) in the Nanotechnology Research Centre (NRC), SRM IST, India. He has obtained his B.Sc., and M.Sc., in Physics from Periyar University, India and a Ph.D. in Engineering from Shizuoka University, Japan. He has published more than 270 research papers international journals and over 200 presentations in national/international conferences. He was awarded the prestigious JSPS Fellowship of the Japanese government (2012–2014). He was received several awards including the Young Researcher Award (Asia Nano 2010), Outstanding Researcher Award (Shizuoka University 2012), and Monbukagusho Japanese government fellowship. His research interests are nanomaterials-based devices such as Dye-sensitized solar cells, Photovoltaics, Photocatalysis, Gas sensors and Thermoelectric materials.

S. Harish is currently working as an Assistant Professor in the Department of Physics and Nanotechnology, SRM IST, India. He has obtained his B.Sc., and M.Sc., in Physics from Madras University, India and a Ph.D. in Physics from SRM University, India. He has published more than 170 research papers international journals and over 100 presentations in national / international conferences. He was awarded the prestigious JASSO Research Fellowship of the Japanese government (2015–2016) & (2017–2018). He was received several awards including the Dean Award for outstanding contribution to Academic research, Shizuoka University, Japan, 2018 and Research grant for overseas travel award, Awarded Murata Science Foundation, Japan GEET 2019, Paris, France. His research interests are nanomaterials-based devices such as Dye-sensitized solar cells, Photovoltaics, Photocatalysis, Gas sensors and Thermoelectric materials.
